text: The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and initiates a medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating the tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and initiates a medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating the tissue layers around the pedicle.
The surgeon exposes lymph nodes colored black from a tattoo placed distal to the tumor in the mid-ascending colon during the right colectomy.
The surgeon exposes lymph nodes colored black from a tattoo placed distal to the tumor in the mid-ascending colon.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and follows the white line laterally, indicating the anatomical boundary during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and follows the white line laterally during the dissection process.
The surgeon points out the inferior vena cava and the ureter, highlighting these anatomical structures to the assistants at the bedside.
The surgeon points out the inferior vena cava and the ureter, highlighting these anatomical structures to the assistants at the bedside.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle at its junction with the superior mesenteric vessels, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle at its junction with the superior mesenteric vessels, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the fatty tissue overlaying the vessels, revealing the blue coloration of the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the fatty tissue overlaying the vessels, revealing the blue hue of the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon ligates the ileocolic artery and continues dissection around the area, focusing on separating tissue layers near the junction with the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon ligates the ileocolic artery and continues with further dissection around the area, focusing on separating tissue layers.
The surgeon exposes the head of the pancreas, visible in the surgical field.
The head of the pancreas becomes visible in the surgical field as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon carefully elevates tissue to expose the lymph nodes, maintaining dissection directly above the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon elevates tissue to expose black-stained lymph nodes while carefully dissecting along the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vein, visible amidst the surrounding tissue, as part of the ongoing dissection near the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vein, visible amidst the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, encountering challenges due to the tumor's adherence to the sidewall. The surgical field is well-exposed, allowing for easier dissection at this stage.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, encountering challenges due to the tumor's adherence to the sidewall. Despite this, the surgeon continues with the dissection using the excellent exposure available.
The surgeon carefully dissects towards the right colic vessels, maintaining proximity to the superior mesenteric vein. The true right colic vessels are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects towards the right colic vessels, maintaining proximity to the superior mesenteric vein. The right colic vessels are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the right colic vessels at their junction with the superior mesenteric vessel, then divides the vein followed by the artery.
The surgeon isolates the right colic vessels at their junction with the superior mesenteric vessel, dividing the vein followed by the artery.
The surgeon continues dissection along the transverse colon mesentery, moving towards the hepatic flexure, while addressing the cecal tumor.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the transverse colon mesentery, moving towards the hepatic flexure, while addressing the cecal tumor.
The surgeon approaches the right branch of the middle colic artery, preparing to take it, while a branch of the loop of Henle is visible but not isolated.
The surgeon identifies a branch of the loop of Henle and proceeds to dissect towards the right branch of the middle colic artery, which is being isolated and taken.
The surgeon places a sponge into the dissected space to protect the duodenum and pancreas, approaching from a super mesocolic angle. The omentum is then split at the level of the falciform ligament.
The surgeon places a sponge into the dissected space to protect the duodenum and pancreas, approaching from a super mesocolic angle.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the falciform ligament, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon disconnects the gastrocolic omentum just distal to the gastroepiploic vessel, ensuring preservation of the vessel during the procedure.
The surgeon disconnects the gastrocolic omentum just distal to the gastroepiploic vessel, ensuring the vessel is preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection, staying just outside the vessel. The cut edge of the gastro-mental vessel is visible to the left of the vessel sealer, indicating the area previously dissected from the medial approach.

The surgeon joins the medial dissection, staying just outside the vessel. The cut edge of the gastro-mental vessel is visible to the left of the vessel sealer, which was previously taken from the medial approach.
The surgeon releases the hepatic flexure, dissecting around the portion of the ascending colon that is adherent to the sidewall, and continues the dissection into the sidewall.
The surgeon releases the hepatic flexure, dissecting around the portion of the ascending colon that is adherent to the sidewall, and continues the dissection into the sidewall.

The surgeon approaches the sidewall from an inferior angle, carefully dissecting to ensure complete removal of any potential tumor invasion, aiming for an R0 resection. The ureter is visually identified and preserved during this process.

The surgeon carefully bites into the sidewall from an inferior approach, aiming for an R0 resection. The dissection is performed with attention to avoiding tumor invasion and ensuring the ureter is not compromised.
The surgeon returns to the inferior approach, maneuvering around the dissected area to complete the procedure.
The surgeon approaches from an inferior angle to complete the dissection, ensuring the final separation of the targeted tissue.
The surgeon divides the small bowel mesentery and checks perfusion, with the stapler positioned at the colon margin before proceeding to take the small bowel.
The surgeon proceeds to take the remaining small bowel mesentery, preparing to check perfusion. The colon margin is divided first, with a stapler in place during the perfusion check.

The surgeon prepares to take the small bowel, using a stapler inserted through the suprapubic port. This port will be upsized to exteriorize the specimen at the end of the procedure, minimizing the risk of hernia by limiting the incision to a single site.

The surgeon performs stapling from the suprapubic port, preparing to upsize it for specimen exteriorization. Stay sutures are placed, and an isoparastaltic anastomosis is aligned. A collotomy and enterotomy are made, indicating the creation of openings in the colon and small intestine.
The surgeon places stay sutures to secure the bowel and aligns an isoparastaltic anastomosis, ensuring proper orientation for the connection between the bowel segments.

The surgeon performs a collotomy followed by an enterotomy, using a vascular load stapler inserted through the suprapubic port to create an anastomosis. The near side stay suture is pulled to facilitate stapler placement, then cut to visualize the near corner. A 3-0 V-Loc suture is used to close the common channel with a baseball suture technique, followed by an imbricating suture.
The surgeon inserts a vascular load stapler through the suprapubic port, using a near side stay suture to facilitate positioning. The stapler is then used to create the anastomosis.

The surgeon cuts the near side stay suture to visualize the corner, then uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to close the common channel with a baseball stitch technique. The suture is applied in an imbricating manner, transitioning around the corner. ICG perfusion is checked again to ensure adequate blood flow, particularly in the right colon.

The surgeon applies a transition suture around the corner of the anastomosis, using a baseball stitch technique to secure the tissue. Indocyanine green (ICG) perfusion is checked to ensure adequate blood flow.
The surgeon navigates around the far corner of the surgical field, using imbricating sutures to secure the tissue layers back down.
The surgeon navigates around the far corner of the surgical site, using an imbricating suture technique to secure the tissue as they proceed back down the line.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis for the right colon, covering the area with residual omentum. The specimen is extracted through a fan and seal incision, revealing its large size.
The surgeon covers the anastomosis site with residual omentum after performing a fan and seal extraction of a large specimen from the right colon.
